titleStmt.title: Letter from Sir Wilfrid Spender to Lady Lillian Spender, 22 July 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Wilfrid Bliss Spender

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: A letter from Sir Wilfrid Bliss Spender (1876-1960) to his wife, Lady Lillian Spender, née Dean, (1880-1968). Spender gives an account of his feelings on being made Lieutenant Colonel and transferred to the General Staff of the 4th Army. He discusses his row with his current Division's General Officer Commanding, his worry that he is deserting his brother Rex, and the poor reputation of the unit to which he is being moved. He also suggests that Lillian manage their accounts after their war, as their joint finances are doing much better under her control than they did under his own.Sir Wilfrid Spender was a member of the British Army until 1913 when controversy surrounding his signature of the Ulster Solemn League and Covenant resulted in his resignation. Spender was called up for military service in 1914 and from 1915 served in the 36th (Ulster) Division. Throughout the war, Spender wrote a collection of letters to his wife Lady Lillian Spender. The letters date mainly from 1915 to 1918, when Sir Wilfrid was serving in France. The letters concern politics, the Irish question, personal life, and Wilfrid's experience of the Great War. Lady Lillian served in the Ulster Volunteer Nursing Corps during the war, and was an organiser of the Ulster Division Comforts Fund.

msIdentifier.repository: Public Record Office of Northern Ireland

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
22nd July My Pr You will have got my news in the other
scrap. I cannot describe my feelings. For some time
I hesitated, & one of the main factors that has decided
me is the unpleasantness. Every one tells me
that it is impossible to refuse & one would not
be allowed to do so, but I wold like to have tried,
but now I feel that the strain would make one's
work largely futile, & might do harm. He is not
pleased & shows that he has not forgiven me so
so there is no living down the misunderstanding. I am off in a few minutes, going quite close
to the district where I was before. It will not
be a bed of roses as this particular lot has a
way of scrapping people in order to cover their
failures. I am an extra so it is not exactly
permanent but it probably means my transfer
to a Division later if I am allowed to survive. It is looked upon as rather better than a GSO1
Division but I don't think it is if it is only
as an extra. I feel like deserting Rex & am afraid he
may feel it too but in some ways it will be
easier for him & for me too. He felt strange &
I in some ways wish he could have had more
time before going into the trenches, but it is
supposed to be a quiet part, & I hope may prove
so at first. Tell Mater & Ed to whom I have not time
to write till I get to the train. I have quite made up my mind that pr
had better manage the accounts afterwards instead
of the b w as they flourish much more under
her control ! Your b w
W B Spender. Though I am only Temporary Lt Col I believe that
in one's letters one is always addressed as Lt Col GS.
I like to feel that, it is due to the success & sacrifice
of our devoted Ulsterman that this reward has been given me.
